web.title: Deploying with the CLI | Railway Docs
web.description: When running railway up from a subdirectory without a path argument, Railway still deploys from the project root. To deploy only a specific ...
web.markdown:
# Deploying with the CLI

On this page

The Railway CLI provides deployment capabilities for both local development workflows and automated CI/CD pipelines.

## [Quick deploy](https://docs.railway.com/cli/deploying\#quick-deploy)

The simplest way to deploy is with `railway up`:

```
# Link to your project first (if not already linked)railway link# Deploy the current directoryrailway up
```

This command scans, compresses, and uploads your app's files to Railway. You'll see real-time deployment logs in your terminal.

Railway will build your code using [Railpack](https://docs.railway.com/builds/railpack) or your [Dockerfile](https://docs.railway.com/builds/dockerfiles), then deploy it.

## [Deployment modes](https://docs.railway.com/cli/deploying\#deployment-modes)

### [Attached mode (default)](https://docs.railway.com/cli/deploying\#attached-mode-default)

By default, `railway up` streams build and deployment logs to your terminal:

```
railway up
```

This is useful for watching the build process and catching errors immediately.

### [Detached mode](https://docs.railway.com/cli/deploying\#detached-mode)

Use `-d` or `--detach` to return immediately after uploading:

```
railway up -d
```

The deployment continues in the background. Check status in the dashboard or with `railway logs`.

### [CI mode](https://docs.railway.com/cli/deploying\#ci-mode)

Use `-c` or `--ci` to stream only build logs and exit when the build completes:

```
railway up --ci
```

This is ideal for CI/CD pipelines where you want to see the build output but don't need to wait for deployment logs. Use `--json` to output logs in JSON format (also implies CI mode).

## [Targeting services and environments](https://docs.railway.com/cli/deploying\#targeting-services-and-environments)

### [Deploy to a specific service](https://docs.railway.com/cli/deploying\#deploy-to-a-specific-service)

If your project has multiple services, the CLI will prompt you to choose. You can also specify directly:

```
railway up --service my-api
```

### [Deploy to a specific environment](https://docs.railway.com/cli/deploying\#deploy-to-a-specific-environment)

```
railway up --environment staging
```

### [Deploy to a specific project](https://docs.railway.com/cli/deploying\#deploy-to-a-specific-project)

Use `-p` or `--project` to deploy to a project without linking:

```
railway up --project <project-id> --environment production
```

**Note:** When using `--project`, the `--environment` flag is required.

## [CI/CD integration](https://docs.railway.com/cli/deploying\#cicd-integration)

### [Using project tokens](https://docs.railway.com/cli/deploying\#using-project-tokens)

For automated deployments, use a [Project Token](https://docs.railway.com/integrations/api#project-token) instead of interactive login. Project tokens are scoped to a specific environment and can only perform deployment-related actions.

```
RAILWAY_TOKEN=XXXXXXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXXXXXXXXXX railway up
```

Some actions you can perform with a project token:

- Deploying code - `railway up`
- Redeploying a deployment - `railway redeploy`
- Viewing build and deployment logs - `railway logs`

### [GitHub actions](https://docs.railway.com/cli/deploying\#github-actions)

Railway makes deployment status available to GitHub, so you can trigger actions after deployments complete.

#### [Post-deployment actions](https://docs.railway.com/cli/deploying\#post-deployment-actions)

Use the `deployment_status` event to run commands after Railway deploys your app:

```
name: Post-Deployment Actionson:  deployment_status:    states: [success]jobs:  post-deploy:    runs-on: ubuntu-latest    if: github.event.deployment_status.state == 'success'    steps:      - name: Run post-deploy actions        if: github.event.deployment.environment == 'production'        run: |          echo "Production deployment succeeded"          # Add your post-deploy commands here
```

See the [GitHub Actions Post-Deploy guide](https://docs.railway.com/guides/github-actions-post-deploy) for more details.

#### [PR environments with GitHub actions](https://docs.railway.com/cli/deploying\#pr-environments-with-github-actions)

Create Railway environments automatically for pull requests:

```
name: Manage PR environments (Railway)on:  pull_request:    types: [opened, closed]env:  RAILWAY_API_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.RAILWAY_API_TOKEN }}  LINK_PROJECT_ID: "your-project-id"  DUPLICATE_FROM_ID: "environment-to-duplicate"jobs:  pr_opened:    if: github.event.action == 'opened'    runs-on: ubuntu-latest    container: ghcr.io/railwayapp/cli:latest    steps:      - name: Link to project        run: railway link --project ${{ env.LINK_PROJECT_ID }} --environment ${{ env.DUPLICATE_FROM_ID }}      - name: Create Railway Environment for PR        run: railway environment new pr-${{ github.event.pull_request.number }} --copy ${{ env.DUPLICATE_FROM_ID }}  pr_closed:    if: github.event.action == 'closed'    runs-on: ubuntu-latest    container: ghcr.io/railwayapp/cli:latest    steps:      - name: Link to project        run: railway link --project ${{ env.LINK_PROJECT_ID }} --environment ${{ env.DUPLICATE_FROM_ID }}      - name: Delete Railway Environment for PR        run: railway environment delete pr-${{ github.event.pull_request.number }} || true
```

**Note:** If you are using a workspace project, ensure the token is scoped to your account, not a specific workspace.

See the [GitHub Actions PR Environment guide](https://docs.railway.com/guides/github-actions-pr-environment) for the complete setup.

## [Redeploying](https://docs.railway.com/cli/deploying\#redeploying)

Redeploy the current deployment without uploading new code:

```
railway redeploy
```

This is useful for:

- Applying environment variable changes
- Restarting a crashed service
- Triggering a fresh build with the same code

## [Deploying a specific path](https://docs.railway.com/cli/deploying\#deploying-a-specific-path)

You can specify a path to deploy:

```
railway up ./backend
```

By default, Railway uses your project root as the archive base. Use `--path-as-root` to use the specified path as the archive root instead:

```
railway up ./backend --path-as-root
```

When running `railway up` from a subdirectory without a path argument, Railway still deploys from the project root. To deploy only a specific directory permanently, configure a [root directory](https://docs.railway.com/deployments/monorepo#root-directory) in your service settings.

## [Ignoring files](https://docs.railway.com/cli/deploying\#ignoring-files)

By default, Railway respects your `.gitignore` file. To include ignored files in your deployment:

```
railway up --no-gitignore
```

## [Verbose output](https://docs.railway.com/cli/deploying\#verbose-output)

For debugging deployment issues:

```
railway up --verbose
```

## [Related](https://docs.railway.com/cli/deploying\#related)

- [CLI Reference](https://docs.railway.com/cli) \- Complete CLI command documentation
- [GitHub Autodeploys](https://docs.railway.com/deployments/github-autodeploys) \- Automatic deployments from GitHub
- [GitHub Actions Post-Deploy](https://docs.railway.com/guides/github-actions-post-deploy) \- Run actions after deployment
- [GitHub Actions PR Environment](https://docs.railway.com/guides/github-actions-pr-environment) \- Create environments for PRs

* * *

web.markdown:
![Thumbnail (1920x1080)](https://i.ytimg.com/vi/CgNvrrLqVko/maxresdefault.jpg)
# [Setting up Railway CLI on windows (Deploy from your local Terminal)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=CgNvrrLqVko)

**Visibility**: Public
**Uploaded by**: [The Cloud Guy](https://www.youtube.com/@TheCloudGuy-o4t)
**Uploaded at**: 2025-05-15
**Published at**: 
**Length**: 03:00
**Views**: 2479
**Likes**: 12
**Category**: People & Blogs

## Description

```
In this quick tutorial, I’ll walk you through setting up the Railway CLI on Windows, so you can easily deploy and manage your projects straight from the command line. Whether you’re building with Node.js, FastAPI, Laravel, or anything in between, this video will help you get started with local-first deployments using Railway.
```

## Transcript

tutorial. So uh I'll be showing you how
to set up u the railway CLA on

just uh
type railway

is a command line interface where you
can interact with your projects. Uh
let's say um you don't want to push um
the project to GitHub. You just want to
deploy it uh locally. And by there I've
been making tutorials. But uh the
tutorials that I've been making, you
have to push
uh the project to GitHub. So let's
install this in
Windows. So you can install this. You
can there are several options.
So just check here. You can use the home
npm scope or directory from the source.
So for me I'm using Windows. So I'm use
this uh the
npm. So it says that I require version
node 16. So I'll just
uh quickly confirm the version that I
have installed.
Yeah. So, I'm currently using the node
version 20. So, this should
work. So, that is it. Uh we have the re
CLA
installed and then we can proceed to the
next bit. Um this is the
authentication this um le login. So
before you use the re you must
authenticate the cla to use your
account. So um I think that's fine
because uh we need to do that.
So railway
login I'll open the
browser and uh that's how you basically
do the
authentication and uh from here on you
can now um start interacting
with your project. So uh in the next
tutorial we will basically deploy a
project um using the CLA without having
to like uh push the code to GitHub. And
uh I think uh that is it.

web.title: railway up | Railway Docs
web.description: Upload and deploy your project from the current directory to Railway. Note: This command uploads and deploys your local code.
web.markdown:
# railway up

On this page

Upload and deploy your project from the current directory to Railway.

**Note:** This command uploads and deploys your local code. To deploy pre-built templates (like databases), use [railway deploy](https://docs.railway.com/cli/deploy) instead.

## [Usage](https://docs.railway.com/cli/up\#usage)

```
railway up [PATH] [OPTIONS]
```

## [Options](https://docs.railway.com/cli/up\#options)

| Flag | Description |
| --- | --- |
| `-d, --detach` | Don't attach to the log stream |
| `-c, --ci` | Stream build logs only, then exit (CI mode) |
| `-s, --service <SERVICE>` | Service to deploy to (defaults to linked service) |
| `-e, --environment <ENV>` | Environment to deploy to (defaults to linked environment) |
| `-p, --project <ID>` | Project ID to deploy to (defaults to linked project) |
| `--no-gitignore` | Don't ignore paths from .gitignore |
| `--path-as-root` | Use the path argument as the archive root |
| `--verbose` | Verbose output |
| `--json` | Output logs in JSON format (implies CI mode) |

## [Examples](https://docs.railway.com/cli/up\#examples)

### [Basic deploy](https://docs.railway.com/cli/up\#basic-deploy)

```
railway up
```

Compresses and uploads the current directory, then streams build and deployment logs.

### [Deploy in detached mode](https://docs.railway.com/cli/up\#deploy-in-detached-mode)

```
railway up --detach
```

Uploads the project and returns immediately without streaming logs.

### [Deploy to specific service](https://docs.railway.com/cli/up\#deploy-to-specific-service)

```
railway up --service backend
```

### [Deploy to specific environment](https://docs.railway.com/cli/up\#deploy-to-specific-environment)

```
railway up --environment staging
```

### [CI/CD mode](https://docs.railway.com/cli/up\#cicd-mode)

```
railway up --ci
```

Streams build logs only and exits when the build completes. Useful for CI/CD pipelines.

### [Deploy a subdirectory](https://docs.railway.com/cli/up\#deploy-a-subdirectory)

```
railway up ./backend
```

## [File handling](https://docs.railway.com/cli/up\#file-handling)

By default, `railway up`:

- Respects your `.gitignore` file
- Respects `.railwayignore` file (Railway-specific ignore patterns)
- Ignores `.git` and `node_modules` directories

Use `--no-gitignore` to include files that would normally be ignored.

## [Exit codes](https://docs.railway.com/cli/up\#exit-codes)

- `0` \- Deployment succeeded
- `1` \- Deployment failed or crashed

## [Related](https://docs.railway.com/cli/up\#related)

- [railway redeploy](https://docs.railway.com/cli/redeploy)
- [railway logs](https://docs.railway.com/cli/logs)
- [Deploying with the CLI](https://docs.railway.com/cli/deploying)

* * *

web.title: Deploying Via Railway  ‍ - Medium
web.description: In this article al explain Step by Step Procedure on how to deploy Ruby and Rails API backend via Railway.
web.markdown:
[Sitemap](https://medium.com/sitemap/sitemap.xml)

[Open in app](https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=com.medium.reader&referrer=utm_source%3DmobileNavBar&source=post_page---top_nav_layout_nav-----------------------------------------)

Sign up

[Medium Logo](https://medium.com/?source=post_page---top_nav_layout_nav-----------------------------------------)

Get app

[Write](https://medium.com/m/signin?operation=register&redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2Fnew-story&source=---top_nav_layout_nav-----------------------new_post_topnav------------------)

[Search](https://medium.com/search?source=post_page---top_nav_layout_nav-----------------------------------------)

Sign up

[Sign in](https://medium.com/m/signin?operation=login&redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2F%40matiesogeoffrey%2Fdeploying-via-railway-b33396faed89&source=post_page---top_nav_layout_nav-----------------------global_nav------------------)

![Unknown user](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:32:32/1*dmbNkD5D-u45r44go_cf0g.png)

# Deploying Via Railway🐱‍🏍

[![Gmatieso](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:32:32/1*G5hsU_cbqK6PDE3qDbSNbQ.jpeg)](https://medium.com/@matiesogeoffrey?source=post_page---byline--b33396faed89---------------------------------------)

[Gmatieso](https://medium.com/@matiesogeoffrey?source=post_page---byline--b33396faed89---------------------------------------)

Follow

5 min read

·

May 16, 2023

68

[Listen](https://medium.com/m/signin?actionUrl=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2Fplans%3Fdimension%3Dpost_audio_button%26postId%3Db33396faed89&operation=register&redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2F%40matiesogeoffrey%2Fdeploying-via-railway-b33396faed89&source=---header_actions--b33396faed89---------------------post_audio_button------------------)

Share

There are many ways when it comes to deploying ruby and rails api backend. But the once I find handy is Render and Railway Deployment. In this article al explain Step by Step Procedure on how to deploy Ruby and Rails API backend via Railway.

When it comes to deploying Railway Application, we can either choose to make the whole deployment via our terminal or onto our browser. Al explains these two methods, though my primary focus will be on the browser mode since it requires less configuration. By browser mode, I simply mean that all our deployment will be initiated by our GitHub which primary connects our railway account. So, lets dive in:

Create an Account and Log to [Railway](https://railway.app/) ,

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![railway login account](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*Zl39DYYTn25Qp1tokBR6iw.png)

After successful account creation and verifying your account, lets Add our DB, kindly note We can choose to add our DB from the browser or from the Terminal, if we choose to add our DB from the terminal then let’s do some few configurations:

Install the Railway CLI using either of the following:

· Installation with brew (only applicable to Mac User)

```
brew install railway
```

· Installation with npm

```
npm I -g @railway/cli
```

Using the 2nd approach assumes that you already have latest node installed in your environment. Why? Lets Think in this way Node is confined with nvm that allow installation of railway ( **kindly** [**chat gpt**](https://chat.openai.com/auth/login) **for more info**)

Upon successful installation of **railway/cli** globally Login using railway login in your terminal which will pop up a browser tab to authenticate into an existing railway session.

From within your project locally, create a new project on Railway using; **railway init**

● Running the above command will prompt you to either create a new project from a template or start an empty project; select, empty.

● You will then be prompted to enter the project name, enter a name of your choice.

● After the two prompts, Railways CLI will automatically set the environment to production, and then a browser window will appear, where you will complete the configurations of the app.

## Adding DB

From the browser window, select add a new service, then database and then PostgreSQL. This will provision a new DB for your project, kindly note you can add as many services as you want but this only applies to those under paid plan. As for Free tier only one service is required

Alternatively, you can simply run **railway add** and select your choice DB from the terminal

## Configuring our Project for Deployment

From the dashboard of the project, add any environment variables, in this case, the PORT variable. Therefore select, Add new variable, then enter the PORT and assign the value of 3000

Create a file with the name Procfile- Note: The name is case-sensitive. In the file add

```
web: rake db:migrate && bin/rails server -b 0.0.0.0 -p $PORT
```

In the above command, we are creating a web instance and then migrating the DB, and finally running the server binding to the IP and getting the port from the environment variable

**NOTE:** The commands in this file are executed every time your app restarts or whenever you try to redeploy.

Create a file **lib/tasks/fake-assets.rake** and add the following code:

```
namespace :assets do
        desc "Should just print stuff and move on. Fake!"
        task :precompile do
            puts "This is a Fake assets:precompile"
        end
end
```

At this point, we are just being clever to override the action done by bundle exec rake assets:precompile. Remember in our full API app we don’t have assets

## Get Gmatieso’s stories in your inbox

Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.

Subscribe

Subscribe

Remember me for faster sign in

Navigate to the database.yml file and change the production setting to point to the remote database.

```
# configuration can be specified
production:
    url:  <%= ENV["DATABASE_URL"]%>
```

Inside **production.rb** and **development.rb** files inside the config/environments folder add

```
config.host<<"<name-of-app>-production.up.railway.app"
```

**Note:** The bracket s <> inside the quotes are meant to mark the beginning and end of your app name, therefore if the name of your app is we-care then the setting should be

```
config.hosts<<"we-care-production.up.railway.app"
```

## Connecting the local and remote app

On the Railway dashboard, select the app you created, then from the settings copy on the project id, and now on the command line within your project run: **railway link <project-id>**

In the above step, you are connecting your local app to the remote app- think of it as git remote add …

NOTE: You can also link to your GitHub repository, and the project will be deployed every time you push the code to it

## Deploy the App

To deploy your app run; railway up

> ● The above command will trigger a build of your project and then push your code to the Railway server.
>
> ● Navigate, to the settings of the app and choose, create domain to get the link to your app
>
> ● After a successful deployment, you can find the link from the dashboard or it will be printed on the terminal. FYI it’s the same link you just used in your environment’s config

To check for logs, navigate to the Railway dashboard, select your app and then select view logs. But because developers like seeing stuff on the terminal, just run railway logs to see the log tail.

## Delete the latest deployment

Run **railway down**

## Running more commands

To seed your data in the database, run;

```
railway run rake db:seed
```

● You can run any command just like you would do locally as long as you begin the command with **railway run**

## References

● CLI \| [CLI \| Railway Docs](https://docs.railway.app/develop/cli)

## Help fix & Hacks 😁

During my deployment i run on a common stuborn error

```
Dockerfile:27
-------------------
25 |     # build phase
26 |     COPY . /app/.
27 | >>> RUN  bundle exec rake assets:precompile
28 |
29 |
-------------------
ERROR: failed to solve: process "/bin/bash -ol pipefail -c bundle exec rake assets:precompile" did not complete successfully: exit code: 1

Error: Docker build failed
```

Incase this error pops on your deployment log kindly fix by creating a **manifest.js** file in **app/assets/config.**

Also after deployment and you get that you running on permission issues based on your deployment log as show above:

```
/bin/bash: line 1: bin/rails: Permission denied
/bin/bash: line 1: bin/rails: Permission denied
```

Run the below command, in your project terminal✔

```
chmod +x bin/rails
```

This might not be the ultimate solution but it worked for me😁. Dont forget to utilize your chat gpt. Byee for now 😜🤞

[![Gmatieso](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:48:48/1*G5hsU_cbqK6PDE3qDbSNbQ.jpeg)](https://medium.com/@matiesogeoffrey?source=post_page---post_author_info--b33396faed89---------------------------------------)

[![Gmatieso](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:64:64/1*G5hsU_cbqK6PDE3qDbSNbQ.jpeg)](https://medium.com/@matiesogeoffrey?source=post_page---post_author_info--b33396faed89---------------------------------------)

web.title: Deploy self-hosted Supabase to Railway with a single command
web.description: For the first time, we can deploy self-hosted Supabase to one of the most exciting up-and-coming cloud platforms with a single command:
